title: manulea annua
intro: Manulea annua, commonly known as Annual Sea Lavender, is a flowering plant species that belongs to the family Scrophulariaceae. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine, and its health benefits include its potential to aid in the treatment of various conditions such as bronchitis, sore throat, and skin irritations. The therapeutic actions of Manulea annua include anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which contribute to its ability to soothe and protect the skin and mucous membranes. The bioactive constituents of Manulea annua include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which are responsible for its medicinal properties.
parts: Manulea annua has many medicinal parts, such as the leaves, flowers, and roots, which are used in traditional medicine. The leaves of the Manulea annua plant contain medicinal constituents like flavonoids and phenolic acids, which have been shown to have anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. The flowers of this plant are rich in essential oils, including linalool and geraniol, which are used to treat various ailments, including fever and respiratory issues. The roots of Manulea annua also contain sesquiterpenes and alkaloids, which have been found to have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory effects, making them useful in treating wounds and skin conditions.
side_effects: Manulea annua can have side effects if used improperly, such as allergic reactions, which may cause symptoms like skin rashes, itching, and swelling. Prolonged use of the plant can also lead to gastrointestinal issues, including nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. In some cases, the plant's active compounds may interact with medications, such as blood thinners, and decrease their effectiveness. Furthermore, Manulea annua can cause liver damage and kidney problems if consumed in excessive amounts or for an extended period.
